title: Bistecca alla fiorentina
subtitle: Florentine T-bone steak, seared hard and served rare
excerpt: A large T-bone or porterhouse steak from the Florentine tradition: seasoned with nothing but salt and pepper, seared hard on a scorching griddle or cast-iron pan, finished with a knob of butter and rested before serving. The steak is served rare to medium-rare; anything further defeats the point.
sourceNotes: Bistecca alla fiorentina is a Florentine preparation documented in Tuscan cookery literature from at least the eighteenth century. The cooking over wood or charcoal is associated with the feast of San Lorenzo in Florence on 10 August. Pellegrino Artusi describes the Florentine method of grilling beef in La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene (1891, Italian public domain).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bring the steak to room temperature for 1 hour before cooking.

content.text: A bistecca alla fiorentina is a T-bone or porterhouse steak cut from the short loin of a Chianina or Maremmana steer, at least 600 g and 4 cm thick, cooked over wood or charcoal and served rare. Outside Florence, a thick-cut T-bone or sirloin steak from a good butcher is what you work with. The cooking principle is the same: very high heat, a hard sear on both sides, a brief rest, served warm and pink throughout.

Season with salt and pepper only, and season just before it goes into the pan — not hours ahead, which draws moisture to the surface and steams rather than sears. The pan must be pre-heated until smoking. Two minutes per side over the highest heat gives the sear; a final minute standing upright on the bone cooks through the thicker end near the T. Rest the steak on a board for at least 10 minutes before cutting.

items.prepNote: T-bone or porterhouse, at least 4 cm thick, at room temperature

content.text: Place a cast-iron skillet or griddle pan over the highest heat for at least 5 minutes until smoking. Do not add oil to the pan. While the pan heats, season the {{beef-sirloin}} of steak generously on both sides with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes and {{black-pepper}} of coarsely ground black pepper.

Place the steak in the hot pan. Do not move it. Sear for 2 to 3 minutes until a deep brown crust has formed. Flip and sear the second side for 2 to 3 minutes. If the steak has a bone, stand it upright on the bone for 1 minute to cook the thick part through. For a rare result the internal temperature should read 50°C; medium-rare is 55°C. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the last 30 seconds and baste the steak with it as it foams.

Transfer the steak to a board and rest for 10 minutes, uncovered. Do not tent it in foil — the crust will steam and soften. Drizzle with the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Carve the steak off the bone and slice thickly against the grain. Serve with lemon wedges.

items.symptom: No sear — steak has steamed rather than browned
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or steak wet when it went in
items.fix: The pan must be smoking before the steak goes in; pat the surface dry; do not crowd the pan

items.symptom: Steak is overdone at the edges but raw at the centre
items.fix: Bring the steak to room temperature for 1 hour before cooking; a cold steak cooks unevenly at high heat
A minimum of 10 minutes resting is non-negotiable for a steak this thick; the internal temperature continues rising and the juices redistribute

content.text: The bistecca alla fiorentina is associated with Florence and with the feast of San Lorenzo on 10 August, when steaks were historically roasted over wood fires in the piazzas of the city. The Chianina breed (large, white, and raised in the Val di Chiana) is the traditional source of the cut; the beef is well-aged and the fat is white rather than yellow. Outside Tuscany this is harder to source, but the method applies to any well-aged thick-cut steak. The rule about serving it rare is not a preference — it is the dish.
